passages.explanation: Paul and Silas pass through Amphipolis and Apollonia and come to Thessalonica, where there's a Jewish synagogue. As is his custom, Paul goes in and for three Sabbaths reasons with them from the Scriptures, explaining and proving that the Messiah had to suffer and rise from the dead. Then he declares: this Jesus I'm proclaiming to you is the Messiah. Some Jews are persuaded and join Paul and Silas, along with a large number of God-fearing Greeks and quite a few prominent women. Paul's method is consistent: start with Scripture, show the pattern of a suffering and rising Messiah, then identify Jesus as that Messiah.

The unbelieving Jews are jealous. They round up some troublemakers from the marketplace, form a mob, and rush to Jason's house looking for Paul and Silas. When they can't find them, they drag Jason and some other believers before the city officials, shouting: these men who have turned the world upside down have come here too, and Jason has welcomed them. They all defy Caesar's decrees, saying there's another king — Jesus. The charges are politically explosive: treason against the emperor. The city officials are disturbed but handle it by making Jason and the others post bond — essentially bail — and then release them. The accusation that they're turning the world upside down is meant as criticism but reads as a compliment.

The believers immediately send Paul and Silas away to Berea by night. On arriving, they go to the Jewish synagogue. Luke praises the Bereans: they're more noble-minded than the Thessalonians because they receive the message with great eagerness and examine the Scriptures every day to see if what Paul says is true. Many believe, including a number of prominent Greek women and men. But when the Thessalonian Jews learn Paul is preaching in Berea, they come and stir up the crowds there too. The believers send Paul to the coast, while Silas and Timothy stay behind. Paul's escorts bring him to Athens and return with instructions for Silas and Timothy to join him as soon as possible.

While Paul waits in Athens, he's deeply distressed to see the city full of idols. He reasons in the synagogue with the Jews and God-fearing Greeks, and daily in the marketplace with whoever happens to be there. Some Epicurean and Stoic philosophers engage him. Some call him a babbler; others say he seems to be advocating foreign gods — because he's preaching Jesus and the resurrection. They bring him to the Areopagus (Mars Hill), the traditional place of philosophical and legal debate, and ask him to explain this new teaching. Luke adds an editorial comment: all the Athenians and foreigners living there spend their time doing nothing but talking about and listening to the latest ideas. Athens is the intellectual capital of the ancient world — and Paul is about to address it.

Paul's Areopagus speech is a masterclass in contextual preaching. He begins with an observation, not a rebuke: people of Athens, I see you're very religious. While walking around, I found an altar with the inscription 'To an Unknown God.' What you worship as unknown, I'm going to proclaim to you. He then builds his case without quoting Jewish Scripture — his audience doesn't know it. The God who made the world and everything in it is Lord of heaven and earth and doesn't live in temples made by human hands. He isn't served by human hands as if he needed anything, because he gives everyone life and breath and everything. From one man he made every nation. He determined their appointed times and boundaries so they would seek him and perhaps reach out and find him — though he's not far from any of us. In him we live and move and have our being — a quote from a Greek poet. We're his offspring — another Greek poet. Since we're God's offspring, we shouldn't think the divine being is like gold, silver, or stone shaped by human design. In the past God overlooked such ignorance, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent. He's set a day when he'll judge the world with justice by a man he's appointed, and he's given proof of this by raising him from the dead. Paul starts with creation, uses their own poets, and ends with resurrection and judgment. No Moses, no Abraham — just the God they almost know.

When they hear about the resurrection of the dead, some sneer — bodily resurrection was absurd to Greek philosophy, which valued the soul's escape from the body. Others say they want to hear more. Paul leaves the Areopagus. A few join him and believe, including Dionysius the Areopagite (a member of the council — a significant convert), a woman named Damaris, and others. Athens doesn't produce a riot or a large church, but it does produce genuine believers. Not every city responds the same way.
